hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
nozzles: Deck gun with 1 3/4-inch smooth bore tip
sceneElements.elevation: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Deck Gun Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Using the FirePumpSim solid-bore flow reference, what is the approximate total deck gun flow?
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The deck gun is operating with a smooth bore tip. Using the smooth bore flow formula, the master stream flow rounds to 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use a handline flow value for a deck gun. A smooth bore master stream is calculated from tip diameter and nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun mounted directly on the apparatus with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip flowing approximately 815 GPM at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. The stream is operating 30 ft above the pump, so add 15 PSI elevation. There is no hose friction loss and no appliance loss. What pump discharge pressure is required?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: A mounted deck gun may have no hose friction loss, but the pump still must cover nozzle pressure plus any stated elevation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not stop at nozzle pressure when the question gives an elevation add-on.

problems.title: Friction Loss
problems.question: The deck gun is mounted directly on Engine 181, so there is no attack hose between the pump and the deck gun nozzle. How much hose friction loss should be added to the deck gun pump-pressure calculation?
formulaBreakdown: Friction loss is calculated for hose lines.
The deck gun is mounted directly on Engine 181.
No attack hose is supplying a remote master stream appliance.
Deck gun hose friction loss = 0 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: A mounted deck gun does not require attack-line friction loss because the stream is supplied directly from the apparatus plumbing.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss for the hydrant supply line as the deck gun friction loss. The question asks for friction loss in the deck gun operation, not the supply line.

problems.title: Alternate Deck Gun Tip Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 switches to a 1½-inch smooth bore deck gun tip operating at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Using the FirePumpSim solid-bore flow formula, what is the approximate flow?
problems.instructorExplanation: This requires using the smooth-bore flow formula instead of repeating a nozzle pressure value.
problems.explainMistake: Do not enter the 80 PSI nozzle pressure as the answer. The question asks for flow in GPM.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying one deck gun flowing 815 GPM. Hydrant static pressure is 80 PSI and residual pressure is 65 PSI. Using the FirePumpSim hydrant-drop rule, how many additional equivalent 815 GPM deck gun streams could likely be supplied?
formulaBreakdown: A 10–24% pressure drop indicates approximately 1 additional equivalent line/stream may be available.
problems.instructorExplanation: This asks for available additional capacity using the hydrant drop rule, not total GPM. A 15 PSI drop from an 80 PSI static pressure is about a 19% drop, which supports about one more equivalent stream in FirePumpSim.
problems.explainMistake: Do not answer with the 815 GPM deck gun flow. First calculate the pressure-drop percentage, then apply the hydrant-drop rule.

problems.title: Additional Attack Line Flow
problems.question: The deck gun is flowing 815 GPM. If one additional 185 GPM attack line is placed in service, what is the new total flow requirement?
formulaBreakdown: New total flow = deck gun flow + attack line flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total fire flow demand is the sum of all flowing devices. Adding one 185 GPM attack line to the 815 GPM deck gun creates a total demand of 1000 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not recalculate the deck gun flow when adding an attack line. Add the new attack-line flow to the existing deck gun flow.
